elements.sent-2411-001: We present a highly parallelizable text compression algorithm that scales efficiently to terabyte-sized datasets.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our method builds on locally consistent grammars, a lightweight form of compression, combined with simple recompression techniques to achieve further space reductions.
elements.sent-2411-003: Locally consistent grammar algorithms are particularly suitable for scaling, as they need minimal satellite information to compact the text.
elements.sent-2411-004: We introduce a novel concept to enable parallelisation, stable local consistency.
elements.sent-2411-005: A grammar algorithm ALG is stable, if for any pattern $P$ occurring in a collection $\mathcal{T =\{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k\}$, the instances $ALG(T_1), ALG(T_2), \ldots, ALG(T_k)$ independently produce cores for $P$ with the same topology.}
elements.sent-2411-006: In a locally consistent grammar, the core of $P$ is a subset of nodes and edges in $\mathcal{T}$'s parse tree that remains the same in all the occurrences of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-007: This feature is important to achieve compression, but it only holds if ALG synchronises the parsing of the strings, for instance, by defining a common set of nonterminal symbols for them.
elements.sent-2411-008: Stability removes the need for synchronisation during the parsing phase.
elements.sent-2411-009: Consequently, we can run $ALG(T_1), ALG(T_2), \ldots, ALG(T_k)$ fully in parallel and then merge the resulting grammars into a single compressed output equivalent to $ALG(\mathcal{T )$.}
elements.sent-2411-010: We implemented our ideas and tested them on massive datasets.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our results showed that our method could process a diverse collection of bacterial genomes (7.9 TB) in around nine hours, requiring 16 threads and 0.43 bits/symbol of working memory, producing a compressed representation 85 times smaller than the original input.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, the exponential growth of data repositories has made it difficult for traditional compression methods to meet the increasing demands for efficiency.

elements.sent-2411-298: We implemented BuildGram and MergeGrams in a tool called LCG.
elements.sent-2411-299: The source code is in C++, and it is available at \url{https://github.com/ddiazdom/lcg .}
elements.sent-2411-300: We support multi-threading in LCG by using MergeGrams as a subroutine of BuildGram.

elements.sent-2411-302: After LCG finishes the construction of the locally consistent grammar, it run-length compresses the grammar and subsequently simplifies it by removing unique nonterminals.

elements.sent-2411-304: After recompressing the grammar, each compressed string becomes a substring in $C[1..k]$, and $O$ stores the position of this substring.

elements.sent-2411-357: We presented a highly parallizable grammar compressor that processes texts at high speeds and has a relatively low memory footprint while achieving high compression ratios.

elements.sent-2411-369: LCG receives as input a string collection $\mathcal{T}$, the number $t$ of allowed working threads, and a threshold $p$ indicating the (approximate) amount of working memory the tool can use.
elements.sent-2411-370: The algorithm begins by initialising an empty grammar $\mathcal{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-371: Then, an I/O thread scans $\mathcal{T}$ from the disk and loads $t$ chunks into $t$ different buffers of up to 200 MB each.
elements.sent-2411-372: The I/O thread inserts the buffers into a queue $Q_i$ as it fills them, and simultaneously, the working threads try to retrieve the buffers from $Q_i$ to process their content using BuildGram.
elements.sent-2411-373: When a working thread finishes processing a buffer, it inserts the buffer into another $Q_o$ that stores the available buffers that the I/O thread can reuse to load new chunks of $\mathcal{T}$.
elements.sent-2411-374: Every working thread has a local grammar that slowly grows as the thread reads more chunks.
elements.sent-2411-375: When the combined memory usage of the working threads exceeds $p$, LCG collapses all the grammars in $\mathcal{G}$ using MergeGrams and flushes the buffers.
elements.sent-2411-376: Now, when a worker thread reads a new buffer, it inserts in its local grammar only those phrases that are not in $\mathcal{G}$, and for those phrases that are in $\mathcal{G}$, it reuses its nonterminals when parsing the chunk's content.
elements.sent-2411-377: The cycle of parsing and merging continues until LCG consumes all the strings in $\mathcal{T}$.
elements.sent-2411-378: We implemented the set of hash functions $\mathcal{H}$ using the hash library xxHash\footnote{\url{https://github.com/Cyan4973/xxHash .}}
elements.sent-2411-379: LCG also implements a mechanism to control the number of pages from $\mathcal{T}$ that the OS can put in the cache.
elements.sent-2411-380: The purpose is to avoid polluting the page cache, especially with massive inputs, and to remove performance bias generated by reading $\mathcal{T}$'s pages from the cache instead of the disk.
elements.sent-2411-381: Specifically, we advise the OS to flush the last pages of $\mathcal{T}$ from the cache each 1 GB.

elements.sent-2411-382: We present compression algorithms designed to process terabyte-sized datasets in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-383: Our approach builds on locally consistent grammars, a lightweight form of compression, combined with simple post-processing techniques to achieve further space reductions.
elements.sent-2411-384: Locally consistent grammar algorithms are suitable for scaling as they need minimal satellite information to compact the text, but they are not inherently parallel.
elements.sent-2411-385: To enable parallelisation, we introduce a novel concept that we call stable local consistency.
elements.sent-2411-386: A grammar algorithm ALG is stable if for any pattern $P$ occurring in a collection $\mathcal{T =\{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k\}$, instances $ALG(T_1), ALG(T_2), \ldots, ALG(T_k)$ independently produce cores for $P$ with the same topology.}
elements.sent-2411-387: In a locally consistent grammar, the core of $P$ is a subset of nodes and edges in the parse tree of $\mathcal{T}$ that remains the same in all the occurrences of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-388: This feature enables compression, but it only holds if ALG defines a common set of nonterminal symbols for the strings.
elements.sent-2411-389: Stability removes this restriction, allowing us to run $ALG(T_1), ALG(T_2), \ldots, ALG(T_k)$ in parallel and subsequently merge their grammars into a single output equivalent to that of $ALG(\mathcal{T )$.}
elements.sent-2411-390: Our experiments showed that our method could process 7.9 TB of bacterial genomes in around nine hours, using 16 threads and 0.43 bits/symbol of working memory, achieving a compression ratio of 85x.
elements.sent-2411-391: Tools like gzip and zstd provide resource-saving simplifications of LZ that offer acceptable trade-offs for smaller inputs, but still struggle with massive repositories.
elements.sent-2411-392: For example, Deorowicz et al.~\cite{d23agc compress pangenomes by partitioning strings and compressing similar segments together using zstd.}
elements.sent-2411-393: Other approaches, Hunt et al.~\cite{atb24 , reorder genomes to improve LZ compression.}
elements.sent-2411-394: Grammar algorithms like RePair~\cite{l2000off and SEQUITUR~\cite{sequitur achieve high compression ratios, but quickly exceed the available memory as the input grows.}}
elements.sent-2411-395: Gagie et al.~\cite{g2019rpair introduced a method using prefix-free parsing~\cite{boucher2019prefix to scale RePair for large inputs.}}
elements.sent-2411-396: Locally consistent grammars~\cite{gawrychowski2018optimal,n2018gr,chri20opt,diaz2021lms,koc24near is a technique that performs rounds of locally consistent parsing~\cite{mehlhorn1997maintaining, sah94sym, muthu00appr, jez2016really, boucher2019prefix, chri20opt, diaz2021lms to compress a text $T[1..n]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-397: This approach recursively segments $T$ based on sequence patterns, producing nearly identical phrases for matching substrings.
elements.sent-2411-398: In the parse tree of a locally consistent grammar, the nodes that cover the occurrences of a pattern $P$ share an area with identical topology and labels.
elements.sent-2411-399: This area is the core of $P$~\cite{sah94sym , and is what makes compression possible.}
elements.sent-2411-400: Locally consistent grammars are simple to construct as, unlike LZ or RePair, they only need local information to break $T$ consistently.

elements.sent-2411-713: In this section, we briefly explain how locally consistent grammars help speed up string processing algorithms (the idea might vary with the application).
elements.sent-2411-714: The most expensive operation in string algorithms is to find matching sequences.
elements.sent-2411-715: We use the fact that a grammar collapses redundant sequence information, so the search space in which a string algorithm has to operate is significantly smaller in the grammar than in the text.
elements.sent-2411-716: Let $\mathcal{G =\{\Sigma, V, \mathcal{R , S\}$ be a locally consistent grammar generating elements in $\mathcal{T}$, and let $ALG(\mathcal{G )$ be a string processing algorithm.}}}
elements.sent-2411-717: As before, we divide $\mathcal{R =\{\mathcal{R ^{1 , \mathcal{R ^{2 , \ldots, \mathcal{R ^{l-1}\}$ according to levels.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-718: We regard the right-hand sides of $\mathcal{R ^{i}$ as a string collection and run (a section of) ALG using $\mathcal{R ^{i}$ as input.}}
elements.sent-2411-719: The process will give some complete answers and some partial answers.
elements.sent-2411-720: We pass the complete answers to the next level $i+1$ and use them as satellite data.
elements.sent-2411-721: When ALG is recursive and returns from the recursion to level $i$, we use the new information to complete what is missing in the partial answers.
elements.sent-2411-722: An example of this idea is the computation of maximal exact matches (MEM).
elements.sent-2411-723: Let $lcp(X, Y)$ be the longest common prefix between $exp(X)$ and $exp(Y)$, with $X,Y \in V^{i-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-724: Similarly, let $lcs(X, Y)$ be the longest common suffix of $exp(X)$ and $exp(Y)$.
elements.sent-2411-725: Assume that we have run a standard algorithm to compute the MEMs on the right-hand sides of $\mathcal{R ^{i}$ and that we found a match $A[j_1..j_2]=B[k_1..k_2]$ between two right-hand sides $A, B$ of $\mathcal{R ^{i}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-726: Completing the MEM requires us to obtain $lcs(A[j_1-1], B[k_1])$ and $lcp(A[j_2+1], B[k_2+1])$.
elements.sent-2411-727: Once we compute all the matches in $\mathcal{R ^{i}$, we use the output so that we can compute $lcs$ and $lcs$ at the next level $i+1$.}
elements.sent-2411-728: We still have to project the MEMs to text positions, but this operation is cheaper than computing MEMs in their plain locations, especially if the text is redundant.
elements.sent-2411-729: The grammar we produce with PBuildGram still requires some modifications to run string algorithms as described above, but the cost of this transformation is proportional to the grammar size, not the text size.
